topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the accounting department hummed with an irritating, relentless drone. Sarah meticulously cross-referenced the quarterly expense reports, her brow furrowed deeper with each line item. Today, however, the usual routine felt tainted. She’d stumbled upon a discrepancy in the departmental budget allocation, a small, almost invisible leak that suddenly seemed enormous. When she finally pulled the comparative salary data for the junior analysts, a cold knot tightened in her stomach. Mark, the newest hire, was earning nearly twenty percent more than her, despite having significantly less experience. Sarah kept her gaze fixed on the screen, her fingers hovering over the mouse, a slight tremor running through her hand. She began double-checking everything, not just for errors, but for patterns, a restless energy making her pace the length of her cubicle wall. She needed confirmation, a solid anchor to steady the unsettling feeling bubbling beneath her skin.

[story 2]

"Just a routine audit," David muttered to himself, tapping his pen against the thick binder. He kept glancing toward Chloe’s desk, a subtle, almost imperceptible shift in his focus every time she walked by. He’d been tracking the payroll discrepancies for weeks, driven by a nagging sense that something wasn't aligning correctly. The numbers were stark: Chloe, the junior associate, was pulling in substantially higher compensation than his own. David leaned back in his chair, forcing a calm expression, but his jaw was tight. He started asking pointed, tangential questions during team meetings, steering the conversation toward compensation structures and performance metrics, watching Chloe’s reaction closely. Every time she offered a breezy, dismissive answer, a flicker of something sharp and uncomfortable sparked behind David’s eyes. He felt compelled to dig deeper, to see if the surface appearance matched the underlying reality.

[story 3]

I watched the data scroll, my breath catching in my throat. It was impossible. I had spent the last hour meticulously sorting through departmental compensation sheets, and the reality staring back at me was jarring. Liam, who had been here for barely six months, was earning a premium that felt entirely unjustified by his output. A tightness built up behind my ribs, a pressure that made breathing shallow. I consciously slowed my typing speed, trying to project an air of detached professionalism, but my eyes kept darting back to the figures. I made a note to review Liam’s project load versus his salary band, a deliberate act of self-control. I felt an urgent need to investigate the source of this disparity, a compulsion to understand the mechanism at play, even if the implications made my skin crawl.
